query: What compound is the best explanation for the 184 nm absorption feature on Rhea?

row.paper: Elowitz, M., et al., 2021. Possible detection of hydrazine on Saturn's moon Rhea. Science Advances, 7(4), eaba5749. DOI:10.1126/sciadv.aba5749
row.query: What compound is the best explanation for the 184 nm absorption feature on Rhea?

tool_input.summary: The 184 nm absorption feature reported on Rhea is best explained by molecular oxygen (O2) on/near the surface (O2 has strong far-UV absorption in the ~175–200 nm Schumann–Runge region). The supporting observations are Cassini UVIS far-UV spectra of Saturn-system targets (including icy satellites such as Rhea).

candidates.title: CASSINI ULTRAVIOLET IMAGING SPECTROGRAPH DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Probed COUVIS_0005 voldesc.cat; it lists CO-S-UVIS-2-SPEC-V1.2 (spectra products) covering Saturn-system UVIS observations. These UV spectra are the direct measurement type used to identify/fit the ~184 nm absorption on Rhea and evaluate candidate absorbers such as O2.

candidates.title: CASSINI ULTRAVIOLET IMAGING SPECTROGRAPH DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Same probed volume (COUVIS_0005) also declares CO-S-UVIS-2-CALIB-V1.2, which is the calibrated UVIS product set. For a science interpretation of an absorption feature (band depth/shape at 184 nm), calibrated products are typically preferred over raw.

candidates.title: CASSINI ULTRAVIOLET IMAGING SPECTROGRAPH DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Probed COUVIS_0007 voldesc.cat; it carries the updated spectra dataset_id CO-S-UVIS-2-SPEC-V1.5. This is the same Cassini UVIS Saturn-tour spectral archive at a later version and is also appropriate for reproducing/confirming the 184 nm absorption feature attribution to O2.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Ring-Moon Systems (RMS) node at https://pds-rings.seti.org/

Directory layout and workflow:
Two parallel trees:
  PDS3 → holdings/volumes/<VOLUME_SET>/<VOLUME>/  (volume-set wraps multiple numbered volumes)
  PDS4 → pds4/bundles/<bundle>/                   (flat list of bundles)

CRITICAL — volume-set explosion warning:
  Volume-sets (e.g. COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx_v1, HSTUx_xxxx_v1.0) contain many numbered
  volumes (often 30-100+). Do NOT probe a volume-set directory directly — pds_probe_datasets
  will recurse into ALL volumes and produce massive redundant output (100+ near-identical results).
  Instead, probe a SINGLE representative volume inside the set, e.g.:
    pds_probe_datasets(paths=['holdings/volumes/COUVIS_0xxx_v1/COUVIS_0009/'], node='rms')
  All volumes in a set share the same dataset_id, so one probe is enough.
  HST volumes are the exception — each volume has a unique dataset_id per HST program,
  but you still should NOT probe the entire set. Pick 1-2 representative volumes.


Naming conventions on RMS:
  PDS3 volumes use uppercase prefixes ending in _xxxx or numbered: COISS_1xxx, COVIMS_0xxx, GO_0017, EBROCC_xxxx.
  Each prefix groups many numbered volumes (e.g. COISS_1xxx contains COISS_1001, COISS_1002, ...).
  PDS4 bundles use lowercase descriptive names: cassini_iss, cassini_uvis, cassini_vims, etc.
Mission/instrument keys (use as filter):
  Cassini → CO* (COISS, COUVIS, COVIMS, COCIRS, CORSS) for PDS3; cassini_* for PDS4
  Voyager → VG_2xxx (imaging), VG_28xx (occultations), VGISS (PDS4)
  Galileo → GO_*
  New Horizons → NHxxLO, NHxxMV
  Hubble → HSTI/HSTJ/HSTU/HSTN (camera era — each volume is a unique HST program)
  Earth-based → EBROCC, ESO_*, RES_*
Cassini UVIS data types (COUVIS volumes):
  COUVIS_0xxx     = raw + calibrated spectra/images (SPEC, SSB, CALIB, CUBE, etc.)
  COUVIS_0xxx_v1  = older v1 publication of the same archive (V1.0 and V1.2 DATA_SET_IDs
                    live in this tree)
  COUVIS_8xxx     = ring stellar/solar occultation profiles
  For UV spectroscopy of surfaces (Rhea, Enceladus, etc.), use COUVIS_0xxx (not _8xxx).
  Early volumes in a COUVIS set can span mission-phase boundaries (Jupiter approach →
  Saturn tour), so the first volume hosting a given DATA_SET_ID is not necessarily
  volume _0001. Use pds_resolve_volume with a dataset_id_hint to find it automatically
  rather than guessing.
Cassini ISS volume-sets:
  COISS_1xxx = cruise-phase EDRs (Earth/Venus/Jupiter; DATA_SET_ID prefixed CO-J/V/E-…)
  COISS_2xxx = Saturn-tour EDRs (the main science dataset; DATA_SET_ID CO-S-ISSNA/ISSWA-…)
  COISS_3xxx = cartographic map products (MIDR)
  COISS_0xxx = calibration files/software
Cassini VIMS volume-sets:
  COVIMS_0xxx = raw image/spectral cubes. DATA_SET_ID convention uses 'QUBE' rather than
    'EDR' in the product-level slot — canonical id is CO-E/V/J/S-VIMS-2-QUBE-V<x>.<y>.
    If a target id uses 'EDR' for VIMS, treat the QUBE form as the equivalent dataset
    hosted at this node.
  COVIMS_8xxx = ring stellar/solar occultation profiles.
Multi-DATA_SET_ID voldescs (common on Cassini):
  COUVIS_*, COCIRS_*, COVIMS_* voldesc.cat files declare DATA_SET_ID as a list (one id per
  product type on the volume — SSB/SPEC/CUBE/CALIB/WAV etc.). pds_probe_datasets now
  exposes the full list in `dataset_ids`. Match the target DATA_SET_ID against this list,
  not only the scalar `dataset_id` field (which is just the first one).


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='rms'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='rms') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='rms', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='rms') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='rms') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='rms', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: Call pds_list_missions(node='rms') to see the 23 instrument/mission filter keys.
Step 2: Identify the SPECIFIC instrument(s) relevant to the query from the mission list:
          - UV spectroscopy/absorption on surfaces → COUVIS (not COISS or HST)
          - Visible imaging of rings/satellites → COISS
          - IR spectral mapping → COVIMS
          - Thermal spectra → COCIRS
        Do NOT explore instruments not mentioned or implied by the query.
Step 3 (PDS3): Call pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='holdings/volumes/', node='rms', filter='<KEY>') with a volume-set prefix from the abbreviation table (e.g. COISS, COVIMS, GO_00, VG_28, NHxxLO).
Step 3 (PDS4): Call pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='pds4/bundles/', node='rms', filter='<key>') — flat list of named bundles (cassini_iss, cassini_uvis, cassini_vims, …).
Step 4: Pick ONE representative volume from each relevant volume-set and probe it. Do NOT probe the volume-set directory itself — see the volume-set explosion warning above. Example: pds_probe_datasets(paths=['holdings/volumes/COISS_2xxx/COISS_2001/'], node='rms')
Step 4b (search by DATA_SET_ID substring inside a large volume-set): When you are
targeting a specific DATA_SET_ID inside a volume-set with many numbered volumes, call
pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path='holdings/volumes/<VOLUME_SET>/', node='rms',
  dataset_id_hint='<DATA_SET_ID fragment>', sample=4) instead of probing volumes one by
one. It probes a hint-ranked sample and returns a `best_match` pointing at the first
child whose `dataset_ids` list contains the requested id. Useful when the first volume
hosting a given id is not _0001 (early volumes can fall in a different mission phase).
Step 5: For PDS4 bundles, call pds_inspect_collections on top 2-3 to get collection LIDs.
Step 6: When the same instrument has BOTH a PDS3 volume-set and a PDS4 bundle, return BOTH candidates. Always scan `dataset_ids` (not only `dataset_id`) since Cassini voldescs ship many ids per volume. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form. Stay under 20 tool calls total.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - opus_search_tool(target=..., instrument=..., time_range=..., ring_geometry=..., observation_type=...): RMS OPUS faceted product search at the observation/granule level.
  - opus_count_tool(...): count results before pulling them.
  - opus_get_metadata_tool(opus_id=...): metadata for one observation.
  - opus_get_files_tool(opus_id=..., product_type=...): file list for one observation, optionally filtered by product type (raw / calibrated / preview / geometry).

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').
What compound is the best explanation for the 184 nm absorption feature on Rhea?

missions.name: COISS
missions.description: Cassini ISS — Imaging Science Subsystem (NAC + WAC). Saturn rings, satellites, atmosphere.

missions.name: COUVIS
missions.description: Cassini UVIS — Ultraviolet Imaging Spectrograph. UV spectroscopy (SPEC), stellar/solar occultations (SSB), calibrated products (CALIB). Covers rings AND satellite surfaces (Rhea, Enceladus, etc.).

missions.name: COVIMS
missions.description: Cassini VIMS — Visual and Infrared Mapping Spectrometer. Rings + satellites.

missions.name: COCIRS
missions.description: Cassini CIRS — Composite InfraRed Spectrometer. Saturn/satellite atmospheres.

missions.name: CORSS
missions.description: Cassini Radio Science Subsystem ring/atmosphere occultations.

missions.name: VG_2xxx
missions.description: Voyager 1/2 imaging (ISS) — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

missions.name: ESO_xxxx
missions.description: European Southern Observatory ground-based ring observations.

missions.name: NHxxLO
missions.description: New Horizons LORRI imaging — Pluto, KBOs, ring search.

missions.name: ASTROM
missions.description: Ground/HST astrometric measurements of irregular satellites.

missions.name: cassini_iss
missions.description: PDS4 bundle: Cassini ISS observations (cruise + Saturn tour).

content.note: Node 'rms' has no mission sub-directories. Use each mission 'name' as the filter keyword: pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='holdings/volumes/', node='rms', filter='<name>').

VOLUME.VOLUME_NAME: CASSINI UVIS SATURN RAW DATA ARCHIVE
VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains a collection of observations taken by the UVIS instrument during the 2003-001...2003-359 time period. UVIS data products include the Spatial-Spectral-Image Cube the Image at One Wavelength, the Solar and Stellar Brightness Time Series and the Spectra data products. The DATA_SET_ID value below specifies the data products on this volume.

VOLUME.VOLUME_NAME: CASSINI UVIS SATURN RAW DATA ARCHIVE
VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains a collection of observations taken by the UVIS instrument during the 2004-140...2005-091 time period. UVIS data products include the Spatial-Spectral-Image Cube the Image at One Wavelength, the Solar and Stellar Brightness Time Series and the Spectra data products. The DATA_SET_ID value below specifies the data products on this volume.

parts.args: {"summary":"The 184 nm absorption feature reported on Rhea is best explained by molecular oxygen (O2) on/near the surface (O2 has strong far-UV absorption in the ~175–200 nm Schumann–Runge region). The supporting observations are Cassini UVIS far-UV spectra of Saturn-system targets (including icy satellites such as Rhea).","candidates":[{"path":"holdings/volumes/COUVIS_0xxx/COUVIS_0005/","dataset_id":"CO-S-UVIS-2-SPEC-V1.2","title":"CASSINI ULTRAVIOLET IMAGING SPECTROGRAPH DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"COUVIS","node":"rms","reasoning":"Stage 1: Probed COUVIS_0005 voldesc.cat; it lists CO-S-UVIS-2-SPEC-V1.2 (spectra products) covering Saturn-system UVIS observations. These UV spectra are the direct measurement type used to identify/fit the ~184 nm absorption on Rhea and evaluate candidate absorbers such as O2."},{"path":"holdings/volumes/COUVIS_0xxx/COUVIS_0005/","dataset_id":"CO-S-UVIS-2-CALIB-V1.2","title":"CASSINI ULTRAVIOLET IMAGING SPECTROGRAPH DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"COUVIS","node":"rms","reasoning":"Stage 1: Same probed volume (COUVIS_0005) also declares CO-S-UVIS-2-CALIB-V1.2, which is the calibrated UVIS product set. For a science interpretation of an absorption feature (band depth/shape at 184 nm), calibrated products are typically preferred over raw."},{"path":"holdings/volumes/COUVIS_0xxx/COUVIS_0007/","dataset_id":"CO-S-UVIS-2-SPEC-V1.5","title":"CASSINI ULTRAVIOLET IMAGING SPECTROGRAPH DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"COUVIS","node":"rms","reasoning":"Stage 1: Probed COUVIS_0007 voldesc.cat; it carries the updated spectra dataset_id CO-S-UVIS-2-SPEC-V1.5. This is the same Cassini UVIS Saturn-tour spectral archive at a later version and is also appropriate for reproducing/confirming the 184 nm absorption feature attribution to O2."}]}
